coredata.dc:title: The potential of pure and atom-decorated AlP nano-sheet as a drug delivery system for procarbazine: A DFT approach
coredata.dc:creator: Kadhim, Mustafa M.
Khamis Mahal, Raffah
Adel, Mohaned
Muhsin Khalaf, Reem
Abdullaha, Sallal A.H.
Haleem Al-Qaim, Zahraa
Hachim, Safa K.
Mahdi Rheima, Ahmed
coredata.dc:description: The impact of decorating the Y (Y = Ti or Sc) atom on the procarbazine (PA) drug delivery performance of an AlP nano-sheet (AlP-NS) was investigated by performing DFT calculations. It was found that the pristine AlP-NS was not a good choice for the drug delivery of PA. Decorating Ti and Sc into the AlP-NS surface increased the adsorption energy of PA from −6.2 to −29.7 and −31.9 kcal/mol, respectively. The Y atom considerably contributed to the generation of virtual orbitals of Y-decorated AlP-NS, which showed that these atoms were more suitable for a nucleophilic attack compared to Al. Finally, the adsorption performance and capacity of PA increased after the decoration of the Y atom, which made the nano-sheet more suitable for the drug delivery of PA. In cancerous cells with a low pH, PA was significantly protonated, which led to the separation of PA from the Y-decorated AlP-NS surface.
coredata.dcterms:subject: AlP nano-sheet
Procarbazine drug
Drug delivery
DFT

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The properties of the AlP-NS and PA
The interaction between PAlP-NS and PA
Decoration of Y on the surface of AlP-NS
The role of solvent in the adsorption (PA/Sc@AlP-NS)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimized structure of the AlP nano-sheet (AlP-NS) and its density of states (DOS). Distance is in Å.
Optimized structure of procarbazine (PA) drug and its HOMO profile.
Optimized structures of PA/AlP-NS complexes. Distances are in Å.
The LUMO profile of Sc-decorated AlP-NS (Sc@AlP-NS) complex.
Optimized structures of PA/Y@AlP-NS complexes. Distances are in Å.
Partial DOS of the most stable PA/Sc@AlP-NS complex.
The HOMO profile of the most stable PA/Sc@AlP-NS complex.
The optimized structure of protonated PA and Sc@AlP-NS, showing separation from each other in the acidic environment. Distances are in Å.

The calculated adsorption energy (Ead) in kcal/mol, HOMO, LUMO energies, and HOMO-LUMO energy gap (Eg) of bare AlP nano-sheet (AlP-NS) and the procarbazine (PA) and AlP-NS complexes in eV. Q is the NBO charge on the molecules.
The calculated adsorption energy (Ead) kcal/mol, HOMO, LUMO energies, and HOMO-LUMO energy gap (Eg) of Y-decorated AlP-NS (Y@AlP-NS) and the PA complexes in eV. Q is the NBO charge on the molecules.

head.ce:title: The potential of pure and atom-decorated AlP nano-sheet as a drug delivery system for procarbazine: A DFT approach

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The impact of decorating the Y (Y = Ti or Sc) atom on the procarbazine (PA) drug delivery performance of an AlP nano-sheet (AlP-NS) was investigated by performing DFT calculations. It was found that the pristine AlP-NS was not a good choice for the drug delivery of PA. Decorating Ti and Sc into the AlP-NS surface increased the adsorption energy of PA from −6.2 to −29.7 and −31.9 kcal/mol, respectively. The Y atom considerably contributed to the generation of virtual orbitals of Y-decorated AlP-NS, which showed that these atoms were more suitable for a nucleophilic attack compared to Al. Finally, the adsorption performance and capacity of PA increased after the decoration of the Y atom, which made the nano-sheet more suitable for the drug delivery of PA. In cancerous cells with a low pH, PA was significantly protonated, which led to the separation of PA from the Y-decorated AlP-NS surface.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Procarbazine (PA) is one of the most commonly prescribed anticancer drugs for treating cancer [1,2]. Also, PA is a cytotoxic chemotherapeutic medication prescribed to treat brain cancers as well as Hodgkin's lymphoma [3–6]. This drug was confirmed for the first time in 1969 and it is on the world health organization (WHO) list of essential medicines [7–12]. It is usually prescribed orally. The side effects associated with PA are depression, tiredness, nausea, and low blood cell counts. It has been shown to harm the baby during pregnancy [13–15]. People with severe kidney or liver problems are not advised to use PA [16–18]. The adsorption of PA on the fullerene (C60) was inspected in gaseous and aqueous solutions by scientists using the density functional theory (DFT) computations. The results revealed that the conductivity, electrophilicity, and reactivity of PA decreased following the substituent reaction. The findings of the study also demonstrated the possibility of using the fullerene (C60) as a promising electrochemical vehicle [19].

Current efforts in drug delivery are vast and include topics such as3D printing, targeted delivery, controlled-release formulations,drug carriers, andnanomedicine [20–24]. Todays, nanotechnology has attracted the attention of many researchers and scientists [25–31]. Graphene (Gr), which is commonly used as a DDS), can be considered a 2D nanostructure [31–34]. However, one of the main drawbacks to the pure Gr as a DDS is that its interaction with various chemicals is often weak [35–37]. Thus, different techniques, such as insertion of impurity atoms into its structure, generation of structural defects, and chemical functionalization, were proposed by researchers [38–42]. Also, impurity atoms such as P and Al have been replaced with several atoms in the structure of nanosheets to synthesize them [43–46]. An AlP Gr-like nanosheet (hereafter referred to as AlP-NS) is a prominent type of nano-structure that has been successfully synthesized [47]. In contrast to Gr, the AlP-NS has a larger energy gap and it has polar Al-P bonds, which implies the potential application of the AlP-NS in semiconductor-based DDSs. Thus far, several studies have been conducted into the interaction between different chemical agents and the AlP-NS [48]. In general, a pure AlP-NS (PAlP-NS) can be used as a suitable DDS because of its high stability and its less tendency for reaction with different chemical agents. Nonetheless, manipulating the structure of the AlP-NS can be conducive to enhancing its sensitiveness to various chemical agents [49–52].

In this work, the interaction between PA and the PAlP-NS and Y (Y = Ti or Sc)-decorated AlP-NS (Y@AlP-NSs) will be inspected by performing DFT calculations in order to make a suitable DDS. Here, Ti and Sc metals were selected because many researchers have demonstrated that doping these metals into the structure of different nanomaterials can significantly increase their sensitivity towards different chemical agents [53]. For example, Cao et al., demonstrated that doping Ti atoms into the AlN nanotubes can significantly increase their sensitivity toward the acrylonitrile gas [54]. Exploring a sensor for detection of toxic phosgene gas, the interaction of pristine and Sc-doped BN nanotubes with the phosgene was investigated by Peyghan et al. [55]. They found that unlike the pristine BN nanotube, the Sc-doped forms can effectively interact with the phosgene molecule, so that their electronic properties and work functions are dramatically changed upon exposure to this molecule.

The density functional B3LYP is capable of providing accurate results about the molecular properties of nanostructures. However, one the main drawbacks to B3LYP is the estimation of dispersion interactions, which were evaluated by including Grimme’s “D” term [56]. The LANL2DZ basis set was used for the Y metal and all computations were undertaken using GAMESS program [57], and 6–311++G** basis set was used for the other atoms. The density-of-states (DOS) diagrams were drawn using the GaussSum [58] program. As mentioned in the literature, one of the common functionals utilized for nano-structures is B3LYP due to its great performance [59,60]. Additionally, two other functionals, namely B97D and M06-2X, were tested. The adsorption energy (Ead) related to PA on the surface of the AlP-NS was computed as follows:
(1)
E
ad
= E
( P A / A l P - N S )
- E
( P A )
- E
( A l P - N S )
+
E
BSSE
where E(PA/AlP-NS) signifies the energy of the AlP-NS that adsorbed a PA molecule. E(PA) signifies energy of PA, and E(AlP-NS) signifies the energy of PAlP-NS. For all interactions, the basis set superposition error (EBSSE) was calculated using the counterpoise method [61,62]. The harmonic frequencies were also computed in order to confirm the correct structures with positive frequencies. The energy gap between the HOMO and the LUMO was computed as follows:
(2)
E g
=
E
LUMO
-
E
HOMO
where EHOMO signifies the energy of the highest occupied molecular orbitals and ELUMO signifies that of the lowest unoccupied molecular orbitals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The properties of the AlP-NS and PA
The interaction between PAlP-NS and PA
ce:section.ce:para: The first step in drug delivery systems is the adsorption of drugs into the carriers. The adsorption of PA onto the surface of the PAlP-NS was explored here by placing PA at different sites, namely the top of the bridge of bonds, above a hexagon center, and on the P or Al. In total, PA was placed on the surface of PAlP-NS in a perpendicular or parallel fashion. As shown in Fig. 3 , three local minima were predicted after optimizing the initial structures. Based on the Ead values of all complexes (CMPs), the adsorption of PA onto the surface of AlP-NS was weak and Ead was in the range of −5.6 to −6.2 kcal/mol (Table 1).

As shown in Fig. 3, in CMP H (the most stable CMP), PA interacted perpendicularly with the surface of AlP-NS at a distance of 3.00 Å for an O atom, with Ead of −6.2 kcal/mol. The popular 6-31G* basis set was also used for the most stable complex (H), which indicated that the Ead slightly changed from −6.2 to −5.9 kcal/mol. Apparently, PA shared its O lone pairs with the Al atom. The Ead of CMP H was around −6.0, and −6.6 kcal/mol at B97D and M06-2X levels of theory. Here, the total natural bond orbital (NBO) charge transported (CT) from PA to the AlP-NS was roughly 0.14 |e|, thus showing that the process of PA adsorption onto the nano-sheet was a physical adsorption. The Eg decreased from 2.618 eV in the PAlP-NS to 2.449 eV in CMP H. The information on the other CMPs is given in Fig. 3 and Table 1. The more stability of the CMP H than the other CMPs can be ascribed to the steric effect and higher structural strain because several atoms interact with the AlP-NS at the same time, and the HOMO of the PA drug was mainly situated on the O atoms. As shown in Table 1, the electronic properties of AlP-NS did not change significantly after the adsorption of PA. This fact and the less negative adsorption energies revealed that the interaction was weak and the adsorption capacity of AlP-NS was very low. Thus, the PAlP-NS cannot be a favorable nanocarrier for PA. In order to solve this problem and to increase the adsorption capacity and strength of the interaction, the Y atom was decorated on the surface of the AlP-NS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Decoration of Y on the surface of AlP-NS
ce:section.ce:para: Some sites on the surface of the AlP-NS were determined to put the Y atom, namely on the middle of an Al-P bond, over the hexagons, and on the top of P or Al atom. After optimizing the initial structures, the most stable CMP was the one in which the Y atom had an adsorption on the center of a hexagon. For instance, Sc@AlP-NS as demonstrated in Fig. 4 . The Ead was −38.3 and −38.7 kcal/mol for the adsorption of Ti and Sc, respectively, showing the strong nature of the interaction. As shown in Table 2 , decoration of the Y atom caused a substantial reduction in the Eg of AlP-NS, which was due to the shift in the level of the LUMO to a lower energy after decorating the metal. The LUMO shape demonstrated that it was mainly limited on the Sc atom (see Fig. 4) after decorating Sc. It was concluded that decorating the Y atom on the surface of AlP-NS changed it into a p-type semiconductor.

In order to find the most stable CMP of PA/Y@AlP-NS, a number of initial adsorption geometries were considered. One CMP with most stability was illustrated in Fig. 5 for each Y@AlP-NS. In CMP PA/Ti@AlP-NS, the Ead of PA on the Ti atom was approximately −29.7 kcal/mol. Dint and CT were 2.81 Å and 0.29 |e|, respectively (Table 2). For the sake of comparison, the Ead for PA on the Ti@AlP-NS using M06-2X and B97D was around −28.4 and −30.1 kcal/mol, respectively. In CMP PA/Sc@AlP-NS, the adsorption of PA on the Sc atom was through its one O atom with Ead of about −31.9 kcal/mol. The Sc…O distance was 2.68 Å, and the CT was 0.33 |e|. The Ead for PA on the Sc@AlP-NS using M06-2X and B97D was around −30.7 and –32.4 kcal/mol, respectively. This demonstrated that at all levels of theory, decorating Ti and Sc increased the electronic sensitiveness of the AlP-NS to PA. The results indicated that the adsorption capacity increased by adding the Y atom to the AlP-NS. Here, this was verified through the electronic property analysis. The results showed that decorating the Sc atom enhanced the adsorption of PA on AlP-NS more than decorating the Ti atom.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The role of solvent in the adsorption (PA/Sc@AlP-NS)
ce:section.ce:para:
The polarizable continuum model (PCM) was exploited to inspect the effect of the H2O solvent (S) on the adsorption of PA on Sc@AlP-NS [63,64], which was done by optimizing the structures of PA, Sc@AlP-NS, and PA/Sc@AlP-NS again in the S. Based on the results, the adsorption became weak and there was a change in Ead from −31.9 to −30.5 kcal/mol. So, the following can be written:
(3)
Δ
E
sol - g a s
=
E
ad
H 2
O
S
-
E
ad
gas
p h a s e
=
1.4
k c a l / m o l
where the difference between the adsorption energies of PA in the gaseous phase and in the S was designated by ΔEsol-gas. ΔEsol (solvation energy) of PA, Sc@AlP-NS, and PA/Sc@AlP-NS CMPs was calculated as follows:
(4)
Δ
E
sol
=
E
sol
-
E
gas
where Esol signifies the energy of a species in the S and Egas denotes the energy of a species in the gaseous phase. The values for ΔEsol of Sc@AlP-NS, PA, and PA/ Sc@AlP-NS, respectively, were −12.9, −7.0, and −13.9 kcal/mol, revealing that the Esol of PA/Sc@AlP-NS was less negative than the sum of Esol of Sc@AlP-NS and that of PA. In fact, Sc@AlP-NS and PA were highly polar with large electric dipole moments, making them highly soluble in the polar S. So, the H2O molecules encircled PA and Sc@AlP-NS systems with a great force and hampered their interaction. Based on our computations, a small change was observed in Eg of Sc@AlP-NS after its interaction with PA in the S (approximately −0.081 eV) in comparison with the gaseous phase. So, the sensing response of Sc@AlP-NS to PA slightly reduced in the S.

The Sc@AlP-NS was demonstrated to be suitable for the adsorption of PA, which was the first and a major step in process of drug delivery. Nevertheless, the release of drugs from the carriers in target cells is a challenging step in drug delivery. Tumor cells have a lower pH in contrast to normal ones, which shows the acidic environment of tissues with the pH of less than approximately 6 [65]. The impact of pH upon the CMP (with most stability) between the Sc@AlP-NS and PA was scrutinized (complex PA/Sc@AlP-NS). H+ species were assumed to have a tendency for attachment to the nucleophilic heads of PA. Hence, the O atom of PA was protonated and the optimization calculations were performed. Therefore, there was a change in the Ead in the acidic environment from −31.9 to −18.7 kcal/mol. Also, there was an increase in the distance between PA and the Sc@AlP-NS to 3.57 Å. Moreover, as illustrated in Fig. 8 , there was a change in the interaction mechanism from covalent bonding into H-bonding, thereby leading to the separation of PA from the carrier through proton attack. It was finally demonstrated that protonated PA was not capable of attaching the carrier, and had to be released.

The attachment of PA to the PAlP-NS and Y@AlP-NS was scrutinized to gain a deeper understanding into the potential use of AlP-NS as a drug carrier. The PAlP-NS had a weak interaction with PA. After the decoration of Y, the most favorable state was demonstrated to be the interaction between the O atom of PA and with the Y atom of the Y@AlP-NS with Ead of approximately −29.7 (for PA/Ti@AlP-NS) and −31.9 (for PA/Sc@AlP-NS) kcal/mol. We predicted a change in the interaction mechanism from covalent bonding in blood to H-bonding in cancer tissues, indicating the possibility of separating PA from Sc@AlP-NS in cancer tissues with a low pH by proton attack. The H-bonding energy was predicted to be approximately −18.7 kcal/mol, which was very low. Finally, our results suggest that Y@AlP-NS (especially Sc@AlP-NS) is a promising nanocarrier for the drug delivery of PA.

Mustafa M. Kadhim: Project administration, Supervision. Raffah Khamis Mahal: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Mohaned Adel: Writing – original draft, Software. Reem Muhsin Khalaf: Data curation, Writing – review & editing. Sallal A.H. Abdullaha: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft. Zahraa Haleem Al-Qaim: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Safa K. Hachim: Resources, Writing – original draft. Ahmed Mahdi Rheima: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.comptc.2023.114048.

ce:section.ce:para:
